items.q: What causes the supply curve to shift?
items.correct: Changes in production costs, technology, taxes/subsidies, number of producers, expectations — anything affecting supply other than the good's own price.
items.misconception: When demand rises, the supply curve shifts right to meet it.
items.random_wrong: Supply shifts only when consumers want more.
items.why: Rising demand causes a movement along the supply curve, not a shift of it. Supply shifts only when something on the supply side changes. Students entangle the two curves.

items.q: What is the difference between revenue and profit?
items.correct: Revenue is total income from sales (price x quantity); profit is what remains after deducting costs from revenue.
items.misconception: Revenue and profit mean the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Revenue is profit before tax and profit is revenue after tax.
items.why: A business with high revenue can still make a loss if costs exceed it. Revenue = sales income; profit = revenue minus costs. Conflating them gives nonsensical analysis.

items.q: What does dependency theory argue about development?
items.correct: Poorer 'periphery' countries are kept underdeveloped by their economic relationships with rich 'core' countries — through unequal trade, debt and historical colonialism. It challenges the view that integration with rich economies automatically helps.
items.misconception: Dependency theory means poor countries are simply lazy.
items.random_wrong: Dependency theory is about households relying on benefits.
items.why: Dependency theory is a structural critique of global capitalism, not a behavioural claim about poor countries. Students sometimes invert the analysis entirely or apply 'dependency' to individuals rather than nations.

items.q: Which of these is a NON-tariff protectionist measure?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Quota on imports. “Import tariff” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A country has comparative advantage when it can produce a good at?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Lower opportunity cost. “Higher cost” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Exact answer: -150. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
